theme: The Office Breathes — Make Every Pixel React to What's Happening Right Now

ideas.title: Coffee Corner Visits — Idle Workers Should Wander to Coffee
ideas.description: Idle workers currently float around the lower floor with generic wander animations, but they never interact with the coffee corner. When a worker is idle, give them a 30% chance per animation cycle of wandering toward the coffee corner (top-right area). Add a new `char--wander-coffee` class with a keyframe that moves the character to ~80% left, ~15% top, pauses for 2s (picking up coffee), then drifts back. While at the coffee corner, show a tiny ☕ emoji bubble above their head (same mechanism as speech bubble but simpler). WHY: The coffee corner is a prop with zero interaction — it's furniture. Making idle workers visit it transforms it into a social space and makes the idle state feel intentional rather than aimless. Every office has that one person who's always at the coffee machine.

ideas.title: Favicon Vibe Indicator — The Browser Tab Should Tell the Story
ideas.description: The browser tab currently shows the default Vite favicon regardless of team state. Use a tiny canvas-generated favicon that changes with vibe: crushing→green circle, on-fire→red circle, in-flow→blue circle, slow-day→gray circle, after-hours→purple circle with moon. In App.jsx, add a useEffect watching the vibe key: create a 32x32 canvas, draw a filled circle with the vibe color, convert to dataURL, and set it as the favicon via `document.querySelector('link[rel=icon]').href = dataURL`. WHY: People who keep the tab open (our target user!) will see the team's state without switching to it. A red dot in the tab bar is an instant 'something's happening' signal. Zero UI footprint, maximum ambient awareness.

ideas.title: Theme Transition Ripple — Dark/Light Toggle Should Feel Satisfying
ideas.description: Currently toggling dark/light mode is an instant swap. Add a CSS transition ripple: when the theme toggle is clicked, use View Transitions API (document.startViewTransition) if available, falling back to a simple 300ms CSS transition on background-color and color properties on :root. For the fancy path: set `::view-transition-old(root)` and `::view-transition-new(root)` with a clip-path circle animation originating from the toggle button's position. For the simple fallback: add `transition: background-color 0.3s ease, color 0.3s ease` to the root custom properties block. WHY: Theme toggles are the most clicked UI element after navigation. Making it feel tactile and smooth turns a utilitarian feature into a moment of delight. Every major design system has this now.

ideas.title: Worker Tooltip Task Preview on Office Floor — Hover to See What They're Building
ideas.description: Working characters already have a `data-tooltip` attribute set to their task, but there's no CSS to actually show it! Add a pure CSS tooltip: `.char[data-tooltip]:hover::after { content: attr(data-tooltip); position: absolute; bottom: 100%; left: 50%; transform: translateX(-50%); background: var(--surface); color: var(--text); border: 1px solid var(--border); border-radius: var(--radius-sm); padding: 4px 8px; font-size: 0.65rem; white-space: nowrap; max-width: 200px; overflow: hidden; text-overflow: ellipsis; z-index: 20; pointer-events: none; box-shadow: 0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.2); }`. WHY: The data is already there — we're just not showing it! This is the lowest-effort, highest-value fix possible. Users hovering over characters on the office floor want to know what they're doing without switching to the dashboard tab.

ideas.title: Error Worker Red Pulse on Office Floor — Errors Should Scream
ideas.description: Workers with status 'error' show an exclamation badge on their avatar, but their desk doesn't react at all. Add a pulsing red glow to desks with errors: `.desk--error .desk__monitor { box-shadow: 0 0 12px rgba(239,68,68,0.6); animation: error-pulse 1.2s ease-in-out infinite; }` `@keyframes error-pulse { 0%,100% { box-shadow: 0 0 8px rgba(239,68,68,0.4); } 50% { box-shadow: 0 0 16px rgba(239,68,68,0.8); } }`. The desk--error class is already being set in the JSX! We just need the CSS. WHY: An error at a desk should make you look. Right now errors are whisper-quiet on the office floor — easy to miss. A red pulse draws the eye immediately, which is exactly what errors should do.

ideas.title: Document Title Updates — Show Team State in the Tab Title
ideas.description: The page title is always 'Bloberto's Office' regardless of what's happening. Add a useEffect in App.jsx that updates document.title based on active workers: `document.title = \`(${workingCount}/${total}) Bloberto's Office — ${vibe.label}\``. When all workers are idle: `document.title = '😴 Bloberto's Office — All quiet'`. When there's an error: prepend '⚠️'. WHY: Combined with the favicon vibe indicator, this turns the browser tab into a complete ambient status display. Users who keep multiple tabs open will see '(3/5) Bloberto's Office — Crushing It 🔥' and know exactly what's happening without clicking.

ideas.title: Konami Code Easter Egg — Reward the Curious
ideas.description: Track keystrokes in a useEffect: ↑↑↓↓←→←→BA. When the sequence is completed, trigger a 3-second 'party mode': every character on the office floor gets a brief confetti burst (CSS-only: spawn 8 small colored squares with randomized animation-delay that fall and fade from above each character). Add a `party-mode` class to `.office-floor` that triggers the confetti keyframe. Remove it after 3s. Could also briefly change all nameplates to silly titles ('Supreme Overlord', 'Bug Whisperer', 'CSS Wizard'). WHY: Easter eggs are the hallmark of products built with love. The Konami code is universally known among developers — our target audience. Someone WILL find it, screenshot it, and share it.

ideas.title: Subtle Floor Reflections — The Office Floor Should Have Depth
ideas.description: The office floor is a flat colored rectangle. Add a subtle CSS pseudo-element reflection under each desk: `.desk::after { content: ''; position: absolute; bottom: -4px; left: 10%; width: 80%; height: 3px; background: radial-gradient(ellipse, rgba(0,0,0,0.15), transparent); border-radius: 50%; }`. Also add a very subtle linear gradient to the floor itself to simulate depth: change `.office-floor` background to include `linear-gradient(to bottom, var(--floor-color) 0%, color-mix(in srgb, var(--floor-color) 85%, black) 100%)`. WHY: Flat surfaces feel like placeholder art. Two CSS pseudo-elements transform the office from a colored box into a space with implied lighting and dimension. Zero performance cost.

ideas.title: Activity Log Sound Toggle — Optional Ambient Notification Pings
ideas.description: Add an optional sound toggle (🔇/🔊 button next to theme toggle). When enabled, play a subtle 'ping' sound via Web Audio API (no audio files needed — generate a short sine wave: `new AudioContext(), createOscillator(), frequency 800Hz, duration 100ms, gain ramp to 0`) when a new activity log entry arrives of type 'complete'. For 'error' type, use a lower frequency (400Hz) two-tone descending beep. Persist the toggle in localStorage as 'bloberto-sound'. Default: off. WHY: For users who keep the tab open while working, audio cues are the ultimate ambient awareness tool. A quiet 'ping' when someone completes a task is satisfying. A low tone on error is immediately attention-grabbing. But it MUST be opt-in — unsolicited sounds are the fastest way to lose a user.

ideas.title: Seasonal Office Decorations — The Office Should Know What Month It Is
ideas.description: Check `new Date().getMonth()` and add subtle seasonal decorations to the office: December → tiny string of colored lights along the top wall (CSS dots with alternating colors and a twinkle animation). October → a small jack-o-lantern prop next to the plants. February → a few tiny heart particles floating near the coffee corner. Summer months → the window shows a brighter, more vivid sky gradient. Each decoration is a conditional render inside Office.jsx based on the current month. Keep them minimal — one small addition per season, not a full redesign. WHY: Seasonal decoration is the single strongest signal that a product is alive and maintained. It tells visitors 'someone cares about this.' It creates return visits ('I wonder what it looks like in December'). And it's pure delight — nobody expects an office dashboard to have Halloween decorations.
